name: Assam Lowlands

staple_foods.name: Assam CTC black tea

primary_metabolites.ascorbic_acid: trace — destroyed in oxidation [black tea — Deka & Bhatt 2013]

key_flavor_bioactives.caffeine_concentration: 2.8-4.5 g/100g dry leaf [Assam CTC — Deka & Bhatt 2013 J Food Sci Tech 50:874; comparable to Darjeeling but higher caffeine in lowland Assam summer flush]
key_flavor_bioactives.polyphenol_content: 12.0-16.0 g/100g dry leaf as catechins [Assam lowland — Kim et al. 2011 Food Chem 129:486; significantly lower than Darjeeling high altitude]
key_flavor_bioactives.tannin_content: 5.0-8.0 g/100g dry leaf [Assam theaflavins+thearubigins — formation during full oxidation; Kim et al. 2011]

terpenes.linalool: 2-8 mg/kg dry leaf [Assam black tea — lower muscatel than Darjeeling; Ravichandran & Parthiban 1998 Food Chem 62:77; oxidation converts terpene alcohols]

organic_acids.malic_acid: 0.2-0.5 g/100g dry leaf [Assam lowland tea — Hilal & Engelhardt 2007 J Consum Prot Food Saf 2:414; lower than high-altitude Darjeeling]
organic_acids.citric_acid: 0.1-0.3 g/100g dry leaf [Assam tea — Hilal & Engelhardt 2007]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 0.4-0.9 g/100g dry leaf as theanine+glutamate [Assam lowland — lower theanine accumulation at warm temperatures; Park et al. 2010 Amino Acids 39:843]

staple_foods.notes: Assam CTC (crush-tear-curl) tea is the low-altitude counterpart to Darjeeling in the tea natural experiment. KEY DIFFERENCES: (1) Cultivar: Assam uses var. assamica (large leaf, warm-adapted) vs Darjeeling var. sinensis (small leaf, cool-adapted) — this is a cultivar confound. (2) Processing: CTC vs orthodox rolling fundamentally changes the oxidation profile — theaflavins and thearubigins replace catechins in the oxidized cup. (3) Catechin content is definitively lower at 100m (12-16 g/100g) than at 2000m Darjeeling (18-22 g/100g). Theanine similarly lower in warm lowland growing conditions. The altitude-catechin gradient is the strongest signal in the RSU-20/21 pair.
data_sources: Deka & Bhatt (2013) — Tea chemistry in northeast India, J Food Sci Tech 50:874
Kim et al. (2011) — Catechin distribution in Indian teas, Food Chem 129:486
Ravichandran & Parthiban (1998) — Volatile compounds in tea, Food Chem 62:77
Hilal & Engelhardt (2007) — Organic acids in tea, J Consum Prot Food Saf 2:414
Park et al. (2010) — L-theanine altitude response, Amino Acids 39:843

staple_foods.name: sticky rice (bora saul)
staple_foods.notes: Assam's native sticky rice (bora saul) is used in traditional pitha cakes, rice beer, and as a staple grain. The waxy starch endosperm (near-zero amylose) gives it characteristic stickiness. GI is higher than long-grain rice.

staple_foods.name: rohu fish

primary_metabolites.protein_content: 16.8-19.5 g/100g [Labeo rohita — Nandakumar et al. 2012 J Food Sci 77:C563]

umami_compounds.glutamate: 0.08-0.12 g/100g free glutamate [freshwater fish — lower than marine fish]
umami_compounds.inosinate: 0.2-0.4 g/100g IMP [freshwater fish IMP — Nandakumar et al. 2012]

staple_foods.notes: Rohu (Labeo rohita) is the dominant freshwater fish of the Brahmaputra river system — among the most commercially important freshwater fish in South Asia. River fish rather than ocean fish; lower omega-3 than marine species but high IMP umami content.
data_sources: Nandakumar et al. (2012) — Nutritional composition of rohu, J Food Sci 77:C563

staple_foods.name: apong rice beer

organic_acids.lactic_acid: 0.3-0.8 g/100g [apong rice beer — LAB component; Tamang et al. 2007 Int J Food Microbiol 118:14]

staple_foods.notes: Apong is the traditional rice beer of the Mising and Bodo tribal communities of Assam — fermented from sticky rice with a dry yeast-mold starter (amao). The fermentation combines yeast, LAB, and molds producing a mildly alcoholic, sour beverage. This is the Assamese equivalent of chicha (RSU-10/18) and tapuy (RSU-12).
data_sources: Tamang et al. (2007) — Microorganisms in traditional fermented foods of northeast India, Int J Food Microbiol 118:14

staple_foods.name: mustard oil

primary_metabolites.oleic_acid: 12 g/100g of total fat [erucic acid dominant at ~40% — mustard oil fatty acid profile]

key_flavor_bioactives.isothiocyanates: allyl isothiocyanate 50-200 mg/100g [mustard oil — formed by myrosinase on sinigrin during pressing; Verkerk et al. 2009 Mol Nutr Food Res 53:S219]

staple_foods.notes: Mustard oil is the dominant cooking fat of northeastern India and the defining flavor of Assamese, Bengali, and Odia cuisines. Cold-pressed mustard oil contains allyl isothiocyanate from glucosinolate hydrolysis — a pungent, horseradish-like compound. This makes mustard oil the highest isothiocyanate fat source in the pilot set (contrasting with olive oil in RSU-15/16 or ghee in RSU-20). Erucic acid (a long-chain monounsaturated fatty acid, ~40%) is food-safety regulated in Western markets.
data_sources: Verkerk et al. (2009) — Glucosinolates in Brassica foods, Mol Nutr Food Res 53:S219

notes: Assam lowlands (Brahmaputra valley, ~100m). Alluvial parent material from the Brahmaputra river system — rich, flat, river-deposited soils. This is the low-altitude counterpart to Darjeeling (RSU-20) for the tea natural experiment. The catechin elevation with altitude is clear (12-16 g/100g Assam vs 18-22 g/100g Darjeeling). Cultivar difference (var. assamica vs var. sinensis) is the main confound. Mustard oil is a unique fat signal not represented elsewhere in the pilot set — isothiocyanate-carrying cooking fat is distinct from olive, palm, lard, and ghee systems.
